title: The document is too long for the context window
problem: You are asked to summarise a 120-page legal contract (~90,000 tokens). Your model has a 32,000 token context window. Describe two architecturally different approaches to solving this, explain the trade-off of each, and say which you would use for a production legal tool and why.
context:
Document: 120-page contract, ~750 words/page
Model context window: 32,000 tokens
Goal: produce a structured summary covering parties, obligations, termination clauses, and liability caps
hints: Approach 1 involves splitting the document and processing pieces sequentially.
Approach 2 involves retrieving only the relevant sections rather than reading everything.
Legal accuracy requirements should drive your production choice.
solution: Approach 1 — Map-reduce summarisation: split the document into 28,000 token chunks, summarise each independently, then summarise the summaries. Trade-off: fast and complete, but the final summary loses cross-chunk relationships (e.g. a liability cap defined in part 1 referenced in part 8). Approach 2 — RAG over the contract: embed all clauses, retrieve only the sections relevant to each summary category (parties, obligations, etc.), inject only those. Trade-off: retrieval misses can cause critical clauses to be omitted entirely. For production legal use I would use map-reduce for completeness, because missing a liability cap is worse than a slightly longer prompt. RAG is better for Q&A over the contract after the fact.
